questions.question: What datasets did 'An Image is Worth 16x16 Words: Transformers for Image Recognition at Scale' use?
questions.gold_answer: ImageNet, ImageNet-21k, CIFAR-100
questions.system_answer: The paper reports pre-training on ImageNet, ImageNet-21k, and JFT-300M. It also evaluates transfer performance on smaller benchmarks including ImageNet, ImageNet-Real, CIFAR-100, and the VTAB suite of 19 tasks.

questions.question: What is the parameter count of the largest variant in 'Training data-efficient image transformers & distillation through attention'?
questions.system_answer: The largest variant listed in the evidence is ViT-L, with 307M parameters. If you mean the largest DeiT variant in this paper, the largest listed is DeiT-B★ with 87M parameters.

questions.question: What architectural innovations does 'Swin Transformer: Hierarchical Vision Transformer using Shifted Windows' introduce?
questions.gold_answer: hierarchical structure, shifted window attention
questions.system_answer: The paper introduces a hierarchical vision Transformer that computes self-attention within non-overlapping local windows, then alternates with shifted window partitioning to create cross-window connections. It also uses patch merging to build multi-scale feature maps, and this design yields linear computational complexity with respect to image size. Relative position bias is another architectural choice highlighted as beneficial.

questions.question: Briefly describe the architecture of 'Emerging Properties in Self-Supervised Vision Transformers'.
questions.gold_answer: DINO is a self-supervised vision transformer training method
questions.system_answer: The paper presents DINO, a simple self-supervised Vision Transformer architecture based on self-distillation without labels: a student network is trained to match a momentum-teacher network across multiple augmented views, with centering and sharpening used to prevent collapse. It is designed to work with both ViTs and convnets, but the paper emphasizes that ViTs with small patches benefit especially strongly.

questions.question: Do papers in this corpus agree on top-1 accuracy on ImageNet?
questions.gold_answer: No—reported top-1 ImageNet accuracy in this corpus varies widely from 1.3% to 91.0% across 594 results.
questions.system_answer: No. The corpus shows a very wide spread in reported ImageNet top-1 accuracy, from 1.3 to 91.0 (stddev 8.69), so the results are not consistent. There are also multiple high-end SOTA claims with different values, including CoCa (finetuned) at 91.0, PaLI-17B at 90.9, and MaxViT-XL at 88.7/88.51, which indicates conflicting SOTA reports rather than a single consistent result.

questions.question: What is the range of top-1 accuracy values reported on CIFAR-100 in this corpus?
questions.gold_answer: The top-1 accuracy on CIFAR-100 in this corpus ranges from 48.5% to 91.88% across 27 results.
questions.system_answer: The reported top-1 accuracy values on CIFAR-100 range from 48.5% to 98.3% across 13 matched results in the corpus.

questions.question: Do papers in this corpus agree on which model holds SOTA on ADE20K mIoU?
questions.gold_answer: No—this corpus does not agree on a single ADE20K mIoU SOTA model, since 7 papers each claim SOTA on the benchmark.
questions.system_answer: No. The corpus does not fully agree on a single ADE20K mIoU SOTA model: the highest reported value in the evidence is InternImage-H at 62.9, while ViT-Adapter-L is also repeatedly claimed as SOTA at 61.5, and SwinV2-G is listed at 59.9. So there are conflicting SOTA claims, with InternImage-H appearing best in this corpus.

questions.question: Do papers in this corpus agree on mIoU values on ADE20K?
questions.gold_answer: No—reported ADE20K mIoU values vary widely across 97 results, from 26.6 to 62.9.
questions.system_answer: No. The ADE20K mIoU values in this corpus are not consistent: they range from 26.6 to 62.9, a spread of 36.3 points with stddev 7.29. The evidence also shows conflicting SOTA claims, with different papers reporting top values such as 62.9 (InternImage-H), 61.5 (ViT-Adapter-L), and 59.9 (SwinV2-G).

questions.question: Do papers in this corpus agree on whether position embeddings are necessary for vision transformers?
questions.gold_answer: Papers disagree on the necessity of position embeddings; some ablations show minimal impact while others demonstrate clear performance drops without them.
questions.system_answer: The corpus does not show a direct disagreement about whether position embeddings are needed; the evidence mostly says they are commonly used to provide positional information because self-attention is permutation-equivariant. One paper frames this as a practical necessity by saying vision transformers “follow the same fashion to imbue the network with positional information,” while another proposes a conditional positional encoding because previous encodings are “predefined and independent of input tokens,” but none of the passages explicitly claim that position embeddings are unnecessary.

questions.question: How did the maximum reported top-1 accuracy on ImageNet change year over year in this corpus?
questions.system_answer: The maximum reported ImageNet top-1 accuracy increased from 85.2 in 2020 to 90.0 in 2021 and 91.0 in 2022, then declined to 87.8 in 2023 and 83.9 in 2024. So the trend was up through 2022, followed by a year-over-year drop in the last two years.

questions.question: What is the trend in average model parameter count by year in this corpus?
